en.extract:
The coleto (Sarcops calvus) is a starling species (family Sturnidae) in the monotypic genus Sarcops. It is endemic to the Philippines. Its natural habitats are tropical dry forest, tropical moist lowland forest, and tropical moist montane forest. In Filipino and Tagalog, this bird is known as kuling or koleto, while in Central Visayas, it is commonly known as the sal-ing.


Description and taxonomy
In 1760 the French zoologist Mathurin Jacques Brisson included a description of the coleto in his Ornithologie based on a specimen collected in the Philippines. He used the French name Le merle chauve des Philippines and the Latin Merula Calva Philippensis. Although Brisson coined Latin names, these do not conform to the binomial system and are not recognised by the International Commission on Zoological Nomenclature. WI 1766 , when the Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus updated his Systema Naturae for the twelfth edition, he added 240 species that had been previously described by Brisson. One of these was the coleto. Linnaeus included a brief description, coined the binomial name Gracula calva and cited Brisson's work. The specific name is from Latin calvus "bald" or "without hair". This species is now the only member of the genus Sarcops that was introduced by the English ornithologist Arthur Walden in 1875. The name combines the Ancient Greek words sarx, sarkos "flesh" and ōps, ōpos "face" or "complexion".


Subspecies
Three subspecies are recognised:

S. c. calvus (Linnaeus, 1766) – north Philippines
S. c. melanonotus (Ogilvie-Grant, 1906) – central and south Philippines
S. c. lowii (Sharpe, 1877) – Sulu Archipelago (southwest Philippines)


Ecology and behavior
Feeds on fruit, berries, small insects and nectar. Forages singly, in pairs and occasionally in small groups of up to 25, in fruiting trees. Breeds from March to September. Nests in cavities in dead trees. Clutch size is typically 2 to 3 eggs.
The Coleto was a recently discovered host of the brood parasitic Asian Koel (Eudynamys scolopaceus) in the Philippines.


Habitat and conservation status
This bird's habitat is primary and secondary forest and scrubland up to 1,000 meters above sea level.
IUCN has assessed this bird as least-concern species as it has a large range and is common throughout. However, deforestation in the Philippines continues throughout the country due to slash and burn farming, mining, illegal logging and habitat conversion. It is widely caught for the pet trade as it has the ability to copy human speech.


References


External links
 Media related to Sarcops calvus at Wikimedia Commons
 Data related to Sarcops calvus at Wikispecies

The '''coleto''' ('''''Sarcops calvus''''') is a [[starling]] [[species]] ([[Family (taxonomy)|family]] Sturnidae) in the [[monotypic]] [[genus]] '''''Sarcops'''''. It is [[endemic]] to the [[Philippines]]. Its natural [[habitat]]s are [[subtropical or tropical dry forest|tropical dry forest]], [[subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest|tropical moist lowland forest]], and tropical moist [[montane forest]]. In [[Filipino language|Filipino]] and [[Tagalog language|Tagalog]], this bird is known as '''''kuling''''' or '''''koleto''''',<ref>{{Cite web |title=Coleto |url=https://www.projectnoah.org/spottings/7476329 |access-date=2023-03-30 |website=Project Noah}}</ref> while in Central Visayas, it is commonly known as the '''''sal-ing'''''.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />

== Description and taxonomy ==
In 1760 the French zoologist [[Mathurin Jacques Brisson]] included a description of the coleto in his ''Ornithologie'' based on a specimen collected in the Philippines. He used the French name ''Le merle chauve des Philippines'' and the Latin ''Merula Calva Philippensis''.<ref name="brisson">{{cite book |last=Brisson |first=Mathurin Jacques |author-link=Mathurin Jacques Brisson |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/36011528 |title=Ornithologie, ou, Méthode contenant la division des oiseaux en ordres, sections, genres, especes & leurs variétés |publisher=Jean-Baptiste Bauche |year=1760 |volume=2 |place=Paris |pages=XXX, Plate 26 fig 2 |language=French, Latin}} The two stars (**) at the start of the section indicates that Brisson based his description on the examination of a specimen.</ref> Although Brisson coined Latin names, these do not conform to the [[binomial nomenclature|binomial system]] and are not recognised by the [[International Commission on Zoological Nomenclature]].<ref name="allen">{{cite journal |last=Allen |first=J.A. |author-link=Joel Asaph Allen |year=1910 |title=Collation of Brisson's genera of birds with those of Linnaeus |journal=Bulletin of the American Museum of Natural History |volume=28 |pages=317–335 |hdl=2246/678}}</ref> WI 1766 , when the Swedish naturalist [[Carl Linnaeus]] updated his ''[[Systema Naturae]]'' for the [[12th edition of Systema Naturae|twelfth edition]], he added 240 species that had been previously described by Brisson.<ref name="allen" /> One of these was the coleto. Linnaeus included a brief description, coined the [[binomial name]] ''Gracula calva'' and cited Brisson's work.<ref>{{cite book |last=Linnaeus |first=Carl |author-link=Carl Linnaeus |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/42946360 |title=Systema naturae: per regna tria natura, secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis |publisher=Laurentii Salvii |year=1766 |edition=12th |volume=1, Part 1 |place=Holmiae (Stockholm) |page=164 |language=Latin}}</ref> The [[Specific name (zoology)|specific name]] is from Latin ''calvus'' "bald" or "without hair".<ref>{{cite web |last=Jobling |first=J.A. |year=2018 |editor1-last=del Hoyo |editor1-first=J. |editor2-last=Elliott |editor2-first=A. |editor3-last=Sargatal |editor3-first=J. |editor4-last=Christie |editor4-first=D.A. |editor5-last=de Juana |editor5-first=E. |title=Key to Scientific Names in Ornithology |url=https://www.hbw.com/dictionary/definition/calva |access-date=12 May 2018 |work=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive |publisher=Lynx Edicions}}</ref> This species is now the only member of the genus ''Sarcops'' that was introduced by the English ornithologist [[Arthur Hay, 9th Marquess of Tweeddale|Arthur Walden]] in 1875.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Walden |first=Arthur |author-link=Arthur Hay, 9th Marquess of Tweeddale |year=1875 |title=A list of the birds known to inhabit the Philippine Archipelago |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30864991 |journal=Transactions of the Zoological Society of London |volume=9 |issue=2 |pages=125–252 [205] |doi=10.1111/j.1096-3642.1875.tb00238.x}}</ref> The name combines the [[Ancient Greek]] words ''sarx, sarkos'' "flesh" and ''ōps, ōpos'' "face" or "complexion".<ref>{{cite web |last=Jobling |first=J.A. |year=2018 |editor1-last=del Hoyo |editor1-first=J. |editor2-last=Elliott |editor2-first=A. |editor3-last=Sargatal |editor3-first=J. |editor4-last=Christie |editor4-first=D.A. |editor5-last=de Juana |editor5-first=E. |title=Key to Scientific Names in Ornithology |url=https://www.hbw.com/dictionary/definition/sarcops |access-date=12 May 2018 |work=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive |publisher=Lynx Edicions}}</ref>

=== Subspecies ===
Three [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name="ioc">{{cite web |year=2018 |editor1-last=Gill |editor1-first=Frank |editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) |editor2-last=Donsker |editor2-first=David |title=Nuthatches, Wallcreeper, treecreepers, mockingbirds, starlings, oxpeckers |url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/nuthatch/ |access-date=12 May 2018 |work=World Bird List Version 8.1 |publisher=International Ornithologists' Union}}</ref>

* ''S. c. calvus'' (Linnaeus, 1766) – north Philippines
* ''S. c. melanonotus'' ([[William Robert Ogilvie-Grant|Ogilvie-Grant]], 1906) – central and south Philippines
* ''S. c. lowii'' ([[Richard Bowdler Sharpe|Sharpe]], 1877) – [[Sulu Archipelago]] (southwest Philippines)

== Ecology and behavior ==
Feeds on fruit, berries, small insects and nectar. Forages singly, in pairs and occasionally in small groups of up to 25, in fruiting trees. Breeds from March to September. Nests in cavities in dead trees. Clutch size is typically 2 to 3 eggs.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Craig |first1=Adrian J. F. |last2=Feare |first2=C. J. |date=2020 |title=Coleto (Sarcops calvus), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/coleto1/1.0/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.coleto1.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

The Coleto was a recently discovered host of the brood parasitic Asian Koel (''Eudynamys scolopaceus'') in the Philippines.<ref>{{Cite journal |year=2023 |title=Brood Parasitism of the Asian Koel Eudynamys scolopaceus on the Tree Cavity-Nesting Coleto Sarcops calvus: First Record in the Philippines |url=https://bioone.org/journals/ornithological-science/volume-22/issue-2/osj.22.175/Brood-Parasitism-of-the-Asian-Koel-Eudynamys-scolopaceus-on-the/10.2326/osj.22.175.short |journal=Ornithological Science |volume=22 |issue=2 |pages=175–178 |doi=10.2326/osj.22.175 |s2cid=260203491 |author1-last=Gicaraya |author1-first=VAG |author2-last=Española |author2-first=CP|url-access=subscription |doi-access=free }}</ref>

== Habitat and conservation status ==
This bird's habitat is primary and secondary forest and scrubland up to 1,000 meters above sea level.

IUCN has assessed this bird as [[least-concern species]] as it has a large range and is common throughout. However, [[Deforestation in the Philippines|deforestation]] in the Philippines continues throughout the country due to slash and burn farming, mining, illegal logging and habitat conversion. It is widely caught for the pet trade as it has the ability to copy human speech.<ref>{{Cite report |url=http://www.iucnredlist.org/details/22710985/0 |title=Sarcops calvus: BirdLife International: The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2016: e.T22710985A94271063 |last=IUCN |date=2016-10-01 |publisher=International Union for Conservation of Nature |doi=10.2305/iucn.uk.2016-3.rlts.t22710985a94271063.en |language=en}}</ref>
